name: Xu, Xu
recent_1: Jung, S., Qing, L., Su, B., & Xu, X. (2026). Toward a Fully Automated NIOSH Lifting Equation Using Computer Vision. Human Factors. DOI: 10.1177/00187208261418858
recent_1_synopsis: This work develops a computer-vision pipeline that automatically extracts the geometric and load inputs required by the revised NIOSH Lifting Equation directly from video of manual lifting, removing the manual measurement burden that limits field ergonomic assessment. It moves automated lifting-risk evaluation toward a turnkey tool deployable on ordinary camera footage.
recent_2: Su, B., Qing, L., Lu, L., Jung, S., Fang, X., & Xu, X. (2025). Enhancing Data Privacy in Human Factors Studies with Federated Learning. Human Factors. DOI: 10.1177/00187208251348025
recent_2_synopsis: This paper applies federated learning to human factors data so that models can be trained across distributed worker datasets without centralizing sensitive personal or biometric information. It addresses a growing tension between data-hungry machine-learning methods and the privacy expectations of human-subjects research in occupational settings.
recent_3: Jung, S., Su, B., Lu, L., Qing, L., & Xu, X. (2024). Video-Based Lifting Action Recognition Using Rank-Altered Kinematic Feature Pairs. Human Factors. DOI: 10.1177/00187208241309748
recent_3_synopsis: This study introduces a video-based action-recognition method that uses rank-altered pairs of kinematic features to identify and classify lifting actions, improving robustness for automated lifting analysis. It supports unobtrusive, marker-less monitoring of manual material handling for musculoskeletal-disorder risk assessment.
seminal_1: Xu, X., McGorry, R. W., Chou, L.-S., Lin, J.-H., & Chang, C.-C. (2015). Accuracy of the Microsoft Kinect™ for measuring gait parameters during treadmill walking. Gait & Posture, 42(2), 145–151. DOI: 10.1016/j.gaitpost.2015.05.002
seminal_1_synopsis: This study quantified how accurately the low-cost, marker-less Microsoft Kinect captures spatiotemporal gait parameters and lower-limb joint angles relative to gold-standard motion capture during treadmill walking. With roughly 315 citations, it is his most-cited paper and became a key reference for the feasibility and limits of consumer depth sensors in human movement measurement.
seminal_2: Xu, X., & McGorry, R. W. (2015). The validity of the first and second generation Microsoft Kinect™ for identifying joint center locations during static postures. Applied Ergonomics, 49, 47–54. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2015.01.005
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper compared the Kinect v1 and v2 sensors for estimating joint-center locations in seated and standing postures, reporting average position errors on the order of 76–87 mm and showing the older generation was somewhat more accurate. With roughly 225 citations, it is a foundational validation study that guided ergonomists in adopting depth cameras for postural assessment.
expertise_overview: Occupational biomechanics and ergonomics with an emphasis on automatically measuring and assessing physical work exposure to prevent musculoskeletal disorders. A central thread is computer vision and machine learning applied to video and depth-camera data—posture estimation, lifting-action recognition, and automated risk scoring (e.g., RULA, the NIOSH Lifting Equation)—as marker-less, low-cost alternatives to laboratory motion capture. He also works with wearable and inertial sensors for activity and distraction recognition, including driving-safety applications. More recent work studies human-robot collaboration, using reinforcement learning and mental-stress modeling to improve workers' musculoskeletal health and safety, and applies privacy-preserving methods such as federated learning to human factors data. Methodologically he draws on optimization-based biomechanical modeling, simulation, and data mining of human daily activities.
notes: Identification confidence: high. The six anchor Human Factors DOIs all resolve via Crossref with 'Xu Xu' as senior (last) author and co-authors (SeHee Jung, Bingyi Su, Liwei Qing, Lu Lu, Ziyang Xie) consistent with the NC State ISE Biomechanics Lab. Identity/affiliation confirmed via NC State ISE faculty page (ise.ncsu.edu/biomechanics/people/xxu/) and Google Scholar profile (user=Sm7mpV0AAAAJ). Citation counts (total ~2,761, h-index 31; seminal_1 ~315, seminal_2 ~225) are from his Google Scholar profile as of June 2026. All cited DOIs (3 recent HF papers + 2 seminal) were verified against Crossref (200 + matching title); the 3 recent papers reuse pre-verified anchor DOIs. No unverifiable citations.
